subject: math
text: 6.1 Introduction to Vectors Scalars Deﬁnition: Measurements that have magnitude only. Scalar variables are written a, b, c, . . . Examples: distance, height, speed, mass, time, temperature, . . . Vectors Deﬁnition: Measurements that have magnitude and direction. Vector variables are written ωa, ωb, ωc, . . . Examples: velocity (10 m/s [N]), acceleration (2 m/s2 [NE]), force (50 N [fwd]), electrical currents (direction = along the wire), magnetic ﬁelds, momentum, electric ﬁelds, displacement, torque, . . . Two ways to describe a vector • Geometric: drawn w.r.t. space/shape/orientation (e.g. N/E/S/W, or “moving forward”). • Algebraic: expressed using components on a standard Cartesian grid. Notation & Magnitude →→↑ AB = vector from point A to point B. →→→↑ AB = vector from B to A, i.e. →→→↑ AB = →→↑ BA. Magnitude (size only): |ωv| or ↓ωv↓ Always: |ωv| = | →ωv|. If ωv is a vector, →ωv has the same size but opposite direction. Equal Vectors & Location Independence Key rule: Any vector with the same magnitude and direction is equal - location does not matter. Rectangle ABCD: →→↑ AB = →→↑ DC, →→↑ AD = →→↑ BC But: →→↑ AB ↔= →→↑ CD, →↑ AC ↔= →→↑

text: Examples: →↑ AE + →→↑ EC = →↑ AC →→↑ DA + →→↑ AB = →→↑ DB →→↑ AB + →→↑ BC = →↑ AC →→↑ AB + →→↑ BD = →→↑ AD →→↑ AB + →→↑ CB = →→↑ DB →→↑ AD + →→↑ CB = ω0 ↗zero vector Important: |→→↑ DA|+|→→↑ AB| ↔= |→→↑ DB| in general (triangle inequality; equality only when collinear). Note: ω0 = zero vector (magnitude 0, direction undeﬁned); not the scalar 0. The sum of two vectors is always a vector. Rectangle ABCD addition examples Basis & Algebraic Vectors Basis = the directions that deﬁne a space. Algebraic vectors are given by their components along basis directions. • 1D: basis is the concept of “1”; every number on a number line is relative to it. • 2D: choose two directions that reach any point. Standard: x-direction ωı = (1, 0) and y-direction ωε = (0, 1) on the xy-grid. • 3D: uses ωı,ωε, and ωk directions. Standard deﬁnitions: ωı = (1, 0), ωε = (0, 1). Any vector →↑ OA is expressed in its basis components: →↑ OA = 2ωı + 3ωε where 2 and 3 are the components of →↑ OA. Non-standard basis

subject: math

Any vector →↑ OA is expressed in its basis components: →↑ OA = 2ωı + 3ωε where 2 and 3 are the components of →↑ OA. text: 6.2 Vector Addition Recall: Geometric vectors are deﬁned by their size and direction using systems such as [NESW] or simply drawing points on a page and connecting them. Algebraic Vectors have their size and direction deﬁned by the Cartesian Plane (x, y) coordinates. The position of the tip (x, y) deﬁnes both the size and direction of the vector with the tail at the origin. Vector addition is an operation we can perform on vectors that represents the result of two vectors positioned tip-to-tail. Property: ωa +ωb = ωb + ωa →Commutative Proof: Tip-to-tail diagram: ωa then ωb vs. ωb then ωa, both giving same resultant Consider shape ABCDEFGH (labelled parallelepiped): ↑↑↓ AB + ↑↑↓ BF = ↑↓ AF ↑↑↓ DB + ↑↑↓ FH = ω0 ↑↑↓ AD + ↑↓ AE = ↑↑↓ AH ↑↓ CA + ↑↑↓ BG = ↑↓ CA + ↑↑↓ AH = ↑↑↓ CH ↑↑↓ BF ↑↑↑↓ EH = ↑↑↓ BF + ↑↑↓ HE = ↑↑↓ DH + ↑↑↓ HE = ↑↑↓ DE ↑↑↓ DB + ↑↑↓ FE + ↑↑↓ GD = ↑↑↓ GD + ↑↑↓ DB + ↑↑↓ BA = ↑↓ GA ↑↑↓ DB + ↑↑↓ HF = ↑↑↓ DB + ↑↑↓ DB = 2↑↑↓

text: Adding Algebraic Vectors Ex: ωa = (7, ↑5), ωb = (↑2, 2) ωa +ωb = # 7 + (↑2), (↑5) + 2 $ = (5, ↑3) Find |ωa +ωb|: |ωa +ωb| = % (5)2 + (↑3)2 = ↔ 34 * Note: |ωa +ωb| ↗= |ωa| + |ωb| |ωa| = % (7)2 + (↑5)2 = ↔ 74 |ωb| = % (↑2)2 + (2)2 = ↔ 8 ↔ 74 + ↔ 8 ↗= ↔ 34 In general: ωa +ωb = (ax, ay) + (bx, by) = (ax + bx, ay + by) Algebraic Vectors - Geo/Alg Conversion Algebraic vectors are written most commonly using the x and y components from the Cartesian grid. Geometric vectors have size and direction. We can convert from one to the other if we overlay the [NESW] system on the Cartesian grid. Alg ↓Geo: 1. Let ωa = (ax, ay) 2. Draw the vector with components to make a right triangle. 3. Find size |ωa| by Pythagorean Thm: |ωa| = % (ax)2 + (ay)2 4. text: 6.3 Multiplication of a Vector by a Scalar Multiplication of a Vector by a Scalar • The direction is not changed • Only the size of the vector is changed In general, vectors are not “multiplied”, but it is okay to multiply a vector by a scalar. However, we cannot add a scalar and vector. Consider ωa + ωa = 2ωa Property: |kωa| = k|ωa|, k →R, k ↑0 Ex: a) ωa = 5ωn [N30→W], then 3ωa = 15ωn [N30→W] b) ωa = (↓1, ↓6), then 3ωa = 3(↓1, ↓6) = (↓1, ↓6) + (↓1, ↓6) + (↓1, ↓6) = (3 ↔(↓1), 3 ↔(↓6)) = (↓3, ↓18) If ωa = (ax, ay), then kωa = (kax, kay) Deﬁnition: Collinear Vectors Two vectors ωa and ωb are said to be collinear if they are in the same or opposite directions. In other words, ωa and ωb are collinear if one vector can be written as a scalar multiple of the other. If ωa = kωb for some k →R, then ωa and ωb are collinear. Think: Must be parallel Ex: 1) (2, ↓3) and (↓12, 18) are collinear because (2, ↓3) = ↓1 6(↓12, 18), or ↓6(2, ↓3) = (↓12, 18)

Prove EF and GH are parallel. Do the same for FH & EG. text: Deﬁnition: Unit Vector A unit vector has a magnitude of 1 in a certain direction. (1, 0) is a unit vector and so is (0, 1). (1, 1) is not a unit vector because the magnitude of (1, 1) is ≃ 12 + 12 = ≃ 2 and the magnitude is obviously not 1. Let ωa be any vector of any size in any direction. Then ˆa is the unit vector in the direction of ωa, and: ˆa = 1 |ωa|ωa Ex: ωa = (3, 4) is not a unit vector since |ωa| = ≃ 32 + 42 = 5. ˆa = 1 |ωa| ωa = 1 5(3, 4) = !3 5, 4 5 " ˆa is the unit vector in the direction of ωa. Check: |ˆa| = #$ 3 5 %2 + $ 4 5 %2 = # 9 25 + 16 25 = # 25 25 = ≃ 1 = 1 ↭ “Collinear”: ˆa = kωb, ˆa = 1 5ωa ↭ 2 〜 < e. g Ʃ(1, ) =( 应,) ァ 悬' ' mite t unit vector in same direction as a

text: Vectors in Three Dimensions Three-Dimensional Vectors ωa = (ax, ay, az) →↑algebraic form A 3-D vector needs 3 components. Use the 3-D Pythagorean theorem to ﬁnd the magnitude: |ωa| = ! a2x + a2y + a2z | ω OA| = " h2 + z2 | ω OA| = " x2 + y2 + z2 h2 = x2 + y2 Drawing a 3-D Point or Vector To draw a vector or point in 3D, we need to use the perspective of a 3D box. x y z To draw a 3D point or vector: 1. Mark the x, y, and z values on their axis. 2. Draw a rectangle to join the y and z value. 3. Draw parallelograms to join x and z and x and y. 4. Complete the box with the missing corner (which is your 3D point). Octants and Coordinate Planes Graph the point A(3, 2, 4). x y z A O 3 2 | ω OA| = ↓ 29 The xyz grid has 8 octants, and there are 3 basic planes that separate those octants. x y z xy-plane (z = 0) x y z yz-plane (x = 0) x y z xz-plane (y = 0)

text: In 3-D: x = 0 is the equation of the yz-plane (a ﬂat surface in R3). y = 0 is the equation of the xz-plane. z = 0 is the equation of the xy-plane. Ax + By + Cz + D = 0 is the general equation of a plane in R3. x y z xy-plane xz-plane yz-plane Identifying Octants Example: Given A(1, 2, 3) - which octant will this be in? - Octant 1 x y z A O Oct. text: By graphing points in R3, we can locate the tip of a vector beginning at the origin. Such a vector is called a position vector. x y z ω OA ω OB ω AB A B ω AB = ω OB →ω OA This vector joining A to B is found using the position vectors ω OA and ω OB (point B minus point A). Standard Basis Vectors Another way of writing algebraic vectors is to use the standard basis vectors. These are unit vectors along the x, y, and z axes. ˆı = (1, 0, 0) ˆε = (0, 1, 0) ˆk = (0, 0, 1) If ω OA = (3, 2, 4): ω OA = 3(1, 0, 0) + 2(0, 1, 0) + 4(0, 0, 1) = 3ˆı + 2ˆε + 4ˆk x y z 3ˆı 2ˆε 4ˆk ω OA A Recall: Use the 3D Pythagorean theorem to ﬁnd the magnitude of a vector. If ωa = (ax, ay, az), then |ωa| = ! a2x + a2y + a2z. Eg: A(4, 1, →2) and B(→1, →3, 3). Find ω AB and | ω AB|. Worked Example x y z A B ω AB = ω OB →ω OA
